Here are the search results for PatternFly 6 design system documentation:

## PatternFly 6 Design System

PatternFly is Red Hat's open source design system. It consists of components, documentation, and code for building enterprise applications at scale.(no content)PatternFly is an open source design system that enables teams to create consistent and scalable enterprise products. PatternFly is sponsored and maintained by Red Hat, but is open to all.

### Key Resources

- **Main Documentation**: [patternfly.org](https://www.patternfly.org/)
- **Design with PatternFly**: [patternfly.org/get-started/design](https://www.patternfly.org/get-started/design/)
- **Upgrade Guide**: [patternfly.org/get-started/upgrade](https://www.patternfly.org/get-started/upgrade/)
- **GitHub Organization**: [github.com/patternfly](https://github.com/patternfly)

### PatternFly 6 Features

PatternFly 6 is built off of a new design token system. For more background and instructions regarding the token system, check out their tokens documentation.(no content)PatternFly 6: Design Tokens & Styles contains all the variables for color, spacing and typography that convey the associated design style for PatternFly.

### Design Tools

Before PatternFly 5, the PatternFly design kit was available within Sketch. They migrated the design system to Figma with the release of the PatternFly 5 library. Sketch is no longer recommended, and the PatternFly 6 design kit is not available in Sketch.(no content)If you are not a Red Hat employee, you will need to create a copy of the PatternFly 6 design file to install the library within your own Figma workspace. Note that you will not be able to receive automatic library updates since you are working from a local copy.

### Upgrading to PatternFly 6

The new design token system in PatternFly 6 changes variable names across PatternFly. Any existing CSS overrides will be targeting outdated styles and will no longer work, so they must be updated or removed.(no content)PatternFly 6 uses rem units for global breakpoint design tokens, rather than pixels.

Based on the search results, here's what I found about PatternFly 6 React components in 2025:

## PatternFly 6 React Components Overview

PatternFly is Red Hat's open source design system. It consists of components, documentation, and code for building enterprise applications at scale.

### Key React Packages

PatternFly is composed of a number of React packages, published in the following npm packages:
- **@patternfly/react-core**: Most React core components
- **@patternfly/react-charts**: PatternFly charting components
- **@patternfly/react-code-editor**: A PatternFly wrapper for the Monaco code editor

### Latest Version

The @patternfly/react-core library provides a set of common React components for use with the PatternFly reference implementation. The latest version is 6.4.0.(no content)There are 195 other projects in the npm registry using @patternfly/react-core.

### Q2 2025 Release Highlights

For the Q2 2025 PatternFly release, progress was made in key initiatives including PatternFly 6 migration enablement, component animations, React 19 support, and the continued evolution of PatternFly AI.(no content)PatternFly now includes support for React 19 across all components and extensions.(no content)As products have continued their PatternFly 6 migration journeys, the team received helpful feedback on the upgrade process, including common roadblocks. They expanded and reorganized the PatternFly 6 upgrade guide to more clearly and comprehensively support the upgrade process.

### PatternFly 6 Upgrade Notes

PatternFly 6 uses rem units for global breakpoint design tokens, rather than pixels. If you have previously implemented any breakpoint logic based on a pixel value, you will need to account for the fact that PatternFly 6 breakpoint tokens use rems.(no content)The new design token system in PatternFly 6 changes variable names across PatternFly. Any existing CSS overrides will be targeting outdated styles and will no longer work, so they must be updated or removed.

### Installation

Using npm, run: `npm install @patternfly/react-core --save` or using yarn: `yarn add @patternfly/react-core`. Each PatternFly component already contains the CSS related to the component.

Here's what I found about PatternFly 6 dark mode theme:

## Enabling Dark Theme in PatternFly 6

Dark theme styles ship with PatternFly CSS by default.(no content)To enable dark theme, add the class `pf-v6-theme-dark` to your application's `<html>` tag.(no content)This class can be added dynamically to toggle dark theme on and off in your application.

## Browser-Based Detection

Dark theme can also be applied based on the browser's `prefers-color-scheme` media query. When using this method, it's recommended to use JavaScript to detect the media query and apply the `pf-v6-theme-dark` class. This allows you to manipulate a single class in order to toggle dark theme based on user preference.

## How Tokens Work with Dark Theme

PatternFly's token resources include styles for both light and dark themes. When dark theme is enabled, your product will automatically pull dark theme tokens in order to adapt visual styles appropriately.(no content)The tokens cover both dark and light themes, making it easier to support both in your product. PatternFly also updated their dark theme handbook to align with their tokens.

## Accessibility

In dark mode, light text is presented on dark backgrounds, and the color palette adapts to maintain a text contrast ratio of at least 4.5:1 and non-text contrast ratio of at least 3:1.

## Best Practices

Most problems with dark theming are due to the use of custom styles, overrides to PatternFly styles, and non-PatternFly components. To ensure consistent styling and behavior when switching themes, try to use PatternFly components as they are.(no content)Use tokens like `var(--pf-t--[version]--global--background--color--primary--default)` for backgrounds instead of static values like `#fff` or `white`. Static values will not change between light and dark theme, but tokens will.

## Handling Images

To create images that adapt to light and dark themes, you can use inline SVGs with fill colors set to PatternFly color tokens (which inherently adapt), or create images with colors that work well with both themes.

For more details, refer to the [PatternFly dark theme handbook](https://www.patternfly.org/developer-resources/dark-theme-handbook/).

Here's information about the PatternFly 6 Page layout component:

## PatternFly 6 Page Component

A page component defines the basic layout of a page, with either vertical or horizontal navigation.

### Key Features

**Page Groups:**
To group page content sections, add 1 or more `<PageGroup>` components to a `<Page>`. The following example adds a group containing `<PageBreadcrumb>` and `<PageSection>` components.

**Group Properties:**
To add additional components and information to a group, you may use the following properties: `isBreadcrumbGrouped` to indicate that a breadcrumb should be in a group, `isHorizontalSubnavGrouped` to indicate that horizontal sub navigation should be in a group, and `additionalGroupedContent` to specify additional group content.

**Width-Limited Sections:**
By default, a page section spans the width of the page. To reduce the width of a section, use the `isWidthLimited` property. To center align width-limited page sections, use the `isCenterAligned` property. When the main content area of a page is wider than the value of a centered, width-limited page section's `--pf-v6-c-page--section--m-limit-width--MaxWidth` custom property, the section will automatically be centered.

**Sidebar:**
To add a vertical sidebar to a `<Page>`, pass a `<PageSidebar>` component into the sidebar property. To render navigation in the sidebar, pass a `<PageSidebarBody>` component to `<PageSidebar>`. The `isSidebarOpen` property helps facilitate the opening and closing of the sidebar and should be 'true' when the sidebar is visible.

**Multiple Sidebar Bodies:**
You can have multiple `<PageSidebarBody>` components inside the `<PageSidebar>` for more customization. You can modify the `<PageSidebarBody>` further by passing `usePageInsets` (to modify the component's insets to match page insets), `isFilled={true}` (to cause the component to grow to fill available vertical space), or `isFilled={false}` (to prevent the component from growing).

**Filling Available Space:**
To change the default behavior, pass `isContentFilled` to the `<Page>` component and `isFilled={true}` to any child `<PageSection>` or `<PageGroup>` component that you want to fill the space. If multiple components are set to fill, then the available space will be split equally between them.

**Padding Options:**
To adjust the padding of a `<PageSection>`, you can pass in different values to the padding property. These values should be aligned to a specific breakpoint: 'default', 'sm', 'md', 'lg', 'xl', and '2xl'. Each breakpoint passed into the property should be given a value of either 'padding' or 'noPadding'. As the page's viewport width increases, breakpoints inherit the padding behavior of the previous breakpoint.

For complete documentation, visit the official PatternFly page component documentation at: https://www.patternfly.org/components/page/


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

Here's what I found about PatternFly 6 Table component for React:

## PatternFly Table Component for React

The Table component lives in its own package at `@patternfly/react-table`.(no content)The Table component takes an explicit and declarative approach, and its implementation closely mirrors that of an HTML table.

### Installation and Basic Usage

It's strongly advised to use the PatternFly base CSS in your whole project:

```jsx
import '@patternfly/react-core/dist/styles/base.css';
import { Table, Thead, Tr, Th, Td, Tbody } from '@patternfly/react-table';
```

### Composable Table Structure

The Table component allows you to compose the table by nesting the relevant `Thead`, `Tbody`, `Tr`, `Th` and `Td` components within it.

### Key Features

- **Selectable Rows**: To make a row selectable, the table needs a selection column. The selection column is just another column, but with selection specific props added. You add it as the first header cell and also as the first body cell for each row.

- **Expandable Rows**: Pass `isExpandable` to Table. Make the first cell in every row an expandable cell by passing `TdExpandType` object to the expand prop on the `Td`. Wrap the content of each child row cell in `ExpandableRowContent`.

- **Editable Rows**: The table supports editable rows where cells in a row can be edited after clicking on the edit icon.

- **Actions Column**: Actions can be added as the last column. The header's last cell is an empty cell, and each body row's last cell is an action cell using the `ActionsColumn` component.

- **Mobile Responsiveness**: Provide `dataLabel` prop to the `Td` components so that in mobile view the cell has a label to provide context.

### React Version Support

PatternFly now includes support for React 19 across all components and extensions. This update is non-breaking, and PatternFly 6 will continue to support React 17 and 18 in line with their support policy.

### Resources

- **Official Documentation**: https://www.patternfly.org/components/table/
- **NPM Package**: https://www.npmjs.com/package/@patternfly/react-table
- **GitHub Repository**: https://github.com/patternfly/patternfly-react

Here are the search results for "PatternFly 6 Masthead Sidebar Navigation":

## PatternFly 6 Masthead and Sidebar Navigation

### Masthead Component
A masthead contains and organizes global properties like a logo, navigation, and settings for easy and consistent access across all pages of an application.(no content)To maintain proper layout and formatting, a `<Masthead>` should contain both a `<MastheadMain>` and `<MastheadContent>` component. Mastheads contain the `<MastheadMain>` that wraps a `<PageToggleButton>` and `<MastheadBrand>`. The `<MastheadBrand>` wraps `<MastheadLogo>`. The masthead also contains the page's header toolbar within `<MastheadContent>`.

### Sidebar Navigation
To add a vertical sidebar to a `<Page>`, pass a `<PageSidebar>` component into the sidebar property. To render navigation in the sidebar, pass a `<PageSidebarBody>` component to `<PageSidebar>`. The `isSidebarOpen` property helps facilitate the opening and closing of the sidebar and should be 'true' when the sidebar is visible.(no content)You can have multiple `<PageSidebarBody>` components inside the `<PageSidebar>` for more customization.

### Horizontal Navigation in Masthead
To add horizontal navigation to the top of a `<Page>`, add the navigation inside of a `<ToolbarItem>` in the `<Toolbar>` that is passed to the `<MastheadContent>` of the `<Masthead>`.

### Managed Sidebar
When the `isManagedSidebar` property is true, it manages the sidebar open/close state, removing the need to pass both `isSidebarOpen` into the `<PageSidebar>` and `onSidebarToggle` into the `<PageToggleButton>`.

### Navigation Variations
Vertical navigation is hierarchical global navigation that displays navigation options from top to bottom on the left side of a screen.(no content)Horizontal navigation is global navigation that displays navigation items from left to right in the application's masthead.

### PatternFly 6 CSS Variables
When the main content area of a page is wider than the value of a centered, width-limited page section's `--pf-v6-c-page--section--m-limit-width--MaxWidth` custom property, the section will automatically be centered.

For more details, you can visit:
- [PatternFly Navigation Design Guidelines](https://www.patternfly.org/components/navigation/design-guidelines/)
- [PatternFly Page Component](https://www.patternfly.org/components/page/)
- [PatternFly Masthead Component](https://www.patternfly.org/components/masthead/)

Here's what I found about the PatternFly 6 Card component for React:

## PatternFly 6 Card Component (React)

PatternFly 6 (Release 6.4.0) defines a card as a content container that displays entry-level information—typically within dashboards, galleries, and catalogs.

### Basic Structure

Basic cards typically have a `<CardTitle>`, `<CardBody>` and `<CardFooter>`. You may omit these components as needed, but it is recommended to at least include a `<CardBody>` to provide details about the card item.

### Card Subcomponents

The Card component includes: Card, CardHeader, CardHeaderActionsObject, CardHeaderSelectableActionsObject, CardTitle, CardBody, CardFooter, and CardExpandableContent.

### Styling Options

You can apply secondary background color styling to a card by setting the `variant` property to `secondary`. You can further modify the styling of the card's content by using various properties.(no content)Most modifiers can be used in combination with each other, except for `isCompact` and `isLarge`, since they are contradictory.

### Header Actions

You can include header actions with the `actions` property of `<CardHeader>`. The following example includes an image using the Brand component, and also includes a kebab dropdown and a checkbox in `<CardHeader>` actions. The actions property for `<CardHeader>` includes the `hasNoOffset` property, which is false by default.

### Expandable Cards

You can place any content that you want to be hidden within the `<CardExpandableContent>` component. An image can be placed in the card header to show users an icon beside the expansion caret. Dividers can be placed between sections of the card.

### Selectable Cards (Tiles)

PatternFly 6 deprecated the tile component in favor of card. For usage information, refer to the cards as tiles example.(no content)Sets of selectable cards can be used as tiles, which are static options that users can select. They can be either single selectable or multi selectable, by passing the `variant` property to the `selectableActions` object. You can also toggle the visibility of the radio or checkbox by passing the `isHidden` property to the `selectableActions` object. To prevent users from selecting more than 1 tile in a set, set variant to "single" within the `selectableActions` object of `<CardHeader>`.

### React Support

PatternFly 6 currently supports React 17 and 18, with support for React 19 coming soon. PatternFly's React library contains a collection of components that you can use to build interfaces with consistent markup, styling, and behavior.(no content)The Card component is available in the `@patternfly/react-core` package, which contains most React core components.

For full documentation, visit the official PatternFly Card component page at: https://www.patternfly.org/components/card/

Based on my search results, here's what I found about PatternFly 6 Badge, Label, and StatusIcon components:

## Badge Component

A badge is an annotation that displays a numeric value.(no content)In PatternFly, components are the basic building blocks of user interfaces. They cannot be broken down into smaller parts. Examples include the button, label, and badge components.(no content)If you want to show a count, use a badge instead of a label.

By default, the menu toggle will display a badge to indicate the number of items that a user has selected.

## Label Component

A label is a descriptive annotation that adds context to an element for clarity and convenience. You can customize labels by adjusting their visual styling or adding functionality to let users click, edit, or remove labels.

Label variants include:
- Status label: Combines color and an icon to communicate a status, like danger, warning, or success.
- Filled label: Can be used for additional emphasis.
- Outlined label: Can be used as a less prominent label style.
- Compact label: Takes up less space than the default label.
- Label with icon: Can utilize a recognizable icon to make the label more clear and identifiable.
- Dismissible label: Can be removed or dismissed by clicking on the "x" close icon.(no content)Labels can be used almost anywhere in a UI. They are commonly used in tables, card views, and filters.

## Status Icons

Status icons convey the status of a data source, system, or similar object. They should not be used without a text label and/or additional context, like within alerts, banners, and empty states. These icons are color coded to help users better understand what a message is trying to communicate.(no content)Status colors and status icons should not be swapped or rearranged. For example, a danger color should not be applied to an info status icon.

## Icon Component

An icon component is a container that supports icons of varying dimensions and styles, as well as spinners.(no content)The Icon component demonstrates various status variants of an icon. Keep in mind that these icons are displayed out of a typical context, and that you should not rely on color alone to convey status to users.

## PatternFly 6 CSS Variables

The badge in a chip has its own CSS variable, --pf-v6-c-chip__c-badge--MarginLeft, defined for the right margin under the CSS Variables section of the chip documentation.(no content)To apply PatternFly styling to the button, add the class pf-v6-c-button. To also apply 'primary' styling to the button and make it a bright blue color, add the pf-m-primary class.

Here's what I found about PatternFly 6 design tokens and CSS variables:

## Overview

Design tokens are variables that store visual design attributes like color, typography, and spacing. Tokens have a name and value that conveys their associated design style, making their purpose clear and recognizable.(no content)PatternFly's tokens are set up as variables and styles within Figma, and are available as CSS variables for development. Tokens are only available as part of the PatternFly 6 release.

## Token System Structure

The PatternFly token system has 3 layers: palette tokens, base tokens, and semantic tokens. Palette tokens use PatternFly color palettes to create a color foundation for other token layers to reference. Base tokens expand on the palette layer to apply PatternFly colors to concepts. They also introduce additional concepts, like spacing and borders. Base tokens are grouped conceptually and named numerically, with no duplicate values in a concept group.

## CSS Variable Naming Convention

Token CSS variables are prefixed with `--pf-t--` and follow the formula `--pf-t--global--concept--property--modifier--state`.

## Examples

### Spacing Tokens
For spacing adjustments, you can use spacers by starting with `pft`, then `sp` for spacers, followed by the spacer size (sm, md, and so on). You'll end up with something like `var(--pf-t--global--spacer--sm)`.

### Color Tokens
When creating custom components or styles, never use hard-coded values like hex codes or color names. Instead, use the appropriate design token, such as `var(--pf-t--global--background--color--primary--default)`. Tokens automatically adapt to different themes, while hard-coded values do not.(no content)For example, to set a disabled background color: start typing `pft` to get tokens, then `back` for background, followed by `dis` for disabled. You'll end up selecting `var(--pf-t--global--background--color--disabled--default)`.

## React Tokens

React tokens can be imported from their file directly: `import t_token_name from '@patternfly/react-tokens/dist/esm/t_token_name'`. React token names are similar to the CSS token names, but use different formatting. Instead of a double-hyphen (`--`), token segments are separated by an underscore (`_`). Additionally the `--pf-t` prefix is replaced with `t`. For example, a CSS variable of `--pf-t--global--spacer--sm` becomes `t_global_spacer_sm` as a React token.

## Theme Support

The token system supports both light and dark themes by default. To enable dark theme, add the class `pf-v6-theme-dark` to your application's `<html>` tag. When dark theme is enabled, your product will automatically pull in dark theme tokens to adapt visual styles appropriately.

## Best Practices

Prioritize semantic tokens: Always use the most relevant semantic token for your use case to ensure the element's purpose is clear. If no semantic token exists, you can fall back to a base token. Never use palette tokens (like `--pf-t--color--blue--20`) directly in your code, as the value is not guaranteed to be consistent across themes.

Based on my search, here's what I found about the PatternFly 6 Toolbar component for React:

## PatternFly 6 Toolbar Component

The Toolbar is available in Release 6.4.0 and is a responsive container that displays controls that allow users to manage and manipulate a data set.

### Key Features

A toolbar can contain multiple toolbar items, like filters and buttons. The `<ToolbarFilter>` component expects applied filters and a delete label handler to be passed in as properties.

### Installation

Using npm, run the following command to install: `npm install @patternfly/react-core --save`. Each PatternFly component already contains the CSS related to the component.

### Toolbar Properties

- **Inset**: To adjust a toolbar's inset, use the inset property. You can set the inset value at multiple breakpoints, including "default", "md", "lg, "xl", and "2xl". Inset values include "insetNone", "insetSm", "insetMd", "insetLg", "insetXl", and "inset2xl".

- **Spacing**: Items are spaced "16px" apart by default and can be modified by changing their or their parents' gap, columnGap, and rowGap properties. You can set the property values at multiple breakpoints, including "default", "md", "lg", "xl", and "2xl".

- **Sticky Toolbar**: To lock a toolbar and prevent it from scrolling with other content, use a sticky toolbar.

- **Color Variant**: To change the background color of a toolbar, use the colorVariant property on the `<Toolbar>`.

- **Multiple Rows**: When all of a toolbar's required elements cannot fit in a single line, you can split toolbar items into multiple rows.

### Toggle Groups (Responsive Behavior)

A toggle group allows you to collapse a set of items into an overlay panel at a certain breakpoint. For example, when a toggle group contains filter controls, its contents will collapse into an overlay panel when the toolbar adapts to a change in the viewport size. The `<ToolbarFilter>` component expects applied filters and a delete label handler to be passed in as properties. Pass in a deleteLabelGroup property to close the entire label group. This property will remove the default clear all filters button.

### PatternFly 6 Changes

In PatternFly 6, the "bulk-select", "overflow-menu", and "search-filter" variants for `<ToolbarItem>` were removed.

## PatternFly 6 Toolbar Component

The Toolbar is available in Release 6.4.0 and is a responsive container that displays controls that allow users to manage and manipulate a data set.

### Key Features

A toolbar can contain multiple toolbar items, like filters and buttons. The `<ToolbarFilter>` component expects applied filters and a delete label handler to be passed in as properties.

### Installation

Using npm, run the following command to install: `npm install @patternfly/react-core --save`. Each PatternFly component already contains the CSS related to the component.

### Toolbar Properties

- **Inset**: To adjust a toolbar's inset, use the inset property. You can set the inset value at multiple breakpoints, including "default", "md", "lg, "xl", and "2xl". Inset values include "insetNone", "insetSm", "insetMd", "insetLg", "insetXl", and "inset2xl".

- **Spacing**: Items are spaced "16px" apart by default and can be modified by changing their or their parents' gap, columnGap, and rowGap properties. You can set the property values at multiple breakpoints, including "default", "md", "lg", "xl", and "2xl".

- **Sticky Toolbar**: To lock a toolbar and prevent it from scrolling with other content, use a sticky toolbar.

- **Color Variant**: To change the background color of a toolbar, use the colorVariant property on the `<Toolbar>`.

Based on the search results, here's what I found about PatternFly 6 and React 19 compatibility:

## React 19 Support in PatternFly 6

PatternFly now includes support for React 19 across all components and extensions, enabling you to benefit from new React features and performance enhancements.(no content)This update is non-breaking, and PatternFly 6 will continue to support React 17 and 18 in line with their support policy.(no content)For the Q2 2025 PatternFly release, they made progress in key initiatives including PatternFly 6 migration enablement, component animations, React 19 support, and PatternFly AI.

## Support Policy

PatternFly supports the 2 most recent versions of React at a time.(no content)The React 19 initiative prioritizes non-breaking changes in PatternFly 6 to enable React 19.

## Migration and Future Plans

Following the initial React 19 compatibility effort, the PatternFly React 19 enhancements Initiative is intended as a breaking change update that will leverage more React features supported in React 18 & 19.(no content)PatternFly is also researching what new features they could adopt to improve the library and what features will need to be deprecated by moving off of React 17.

## Extensions

The react-virtualized-extension package added React 19 support in version 6.1.0.

In summary, React 19 is now fully supported in PatternFly 6 as of Q2 2025, and it's a non-breaking change that maintains backward compatibility with React 17 and 18.

Based on the search results, here's information about PatternFly form components including TextInput, Checkbox, and DatePicker:

## PatternFly Form Components

### TextInput
A text input component allows users to input short text.(no content)A text input is used to gather free-form text from a user. You can set a value to indicate if the input is modified to show validation state - if set to success, input will be modified to indicate valid state, and if set to warning, input will be modified to indicate warning state.

### TextInputGroup
To help users identify the purpose of an input group, you can add an icon by importing your icon and passing it to icon within `<TextInputGroupMain>`. You can also add additional actions or utilities via `<TextInputGroupUtilities>`, such as a clear button that allows users to remove their previous input.(no content)You can add a validation status to a `<TextInputGroup>` by passing the validated property with a value of either "success", "warning", or "error".

### Checkbox
Use checkboxes to enable users to select all, some, or none of the available options from a list of up to 5 items.(no content)Checkbox labels fall to the right of the checkbox control. If placed vertically, all checkbox controls should be aligned with the left edge of other controls or input fields in the form or on the page.(no content)You also have the option to use progressive disclosure by nesting other controls underneath a checkbox. The nested options would be enabled or disabled when the user selects or deselects a checkbox.(no content)The main difference between a switch and a checkbox is that a switch changes an option and saves it simultaneously, while checkboxes require a separate action such as pressing a "Submit" or "Save" button to save the selection.

### DatePicker
PatternFly is Red Hat's open source design system. It consists of components, documentation, and code for building enterprise applications at scale.(no content)If users must enter specifically-formatted data into the Text Field, use syntax hints and field validation to prevent data entry errors. Use bound-entry controls for entering special data types, such as dates, times, and other numeric data.(no content)Bound-entry controls constrain user input and combine keyboard and mouse interaction. These controls are designed to only allow valid inputs, and as a result, field validation is unnecessary.

### Form Design Guidelines
Always provide labels for text and data inputs so that users understand the information each form field asks of them. Default to top-aligned labels unless you're working within limited vertical space, then use left-aligned labels. Maintain consistent form label alignment throughout your application or interface.(no content)Always add 24px of spacing underneath each form input. If a form input includes helper text, this 24px spacing should start below the helper text. Spacing between data inputs like checkboxes and radio buttons should also be 24px when on the same line or stacked on one another.

For more detailed documentation, you can visit:
- TextInput: https://www.patternfly.org/components/forms/text-input/
- Checkbox: https://www.patternfly.org/components/forms/checkbox/
- DatePicker: https://www.patternfly.org/components/date-and-time/date-picker/

Based on my search, here's what I found about PatternFly 6 Button, Menu, Dropdown, and Select components:

## PatternFly Dropdown Component
A `<Dropdown>` uses a menu toggle, which is passed to the toggle property, as well as a `<DropdownList>` with multiple `<DropdownItem>` components.(no content)To allow a dropdown to change the open state of the menu, pass the `isOpen` property into the `onOpenChange` callback.

Key features:
- Dropdown items may use the `isDisabled` property to disable an item, and use the `onClick` property to trigger a callback for an action.
- To style a dropdown menu's toggle with a kebab icon, pass a "plain" `<MenuToggle>` to the toggle property. When there isn't enough space for a labeled button, a kebab icon can be used to toggle the dropdown menu open or closed.
- To group sets of related dropdown items together, use a `<DropdownGroup>`.

## PatternFly Select Component
A select component is a menu that enables users to select 1 or more items from a list.(no content)Select builds off of the menu component suite to adapt commonly used properties and functions to create a select menu. See the menu documentation for a full list of properties that may be used to further customize a select menu.

Key features:
- To let users select a single item from a list, use a single select menu. You can add multiple `<SelectOption>` components to build out a list of menu items.
- To create a checkbox select, pass `role="menu"` to the `<Select>`, and `hasCheckbox` into each `<SelectOption>` component. To indicate that an option is selected, use the `isSelected` property. By default, the menu toggle will display a badge to indicate the number of items that a user has selected.
- Typeahead is a select variant that replaces the typical button toggle for opening the select menu with a text input and button toggle combo. As a user enters characters into the text input, the menu options will be filtered to match. To make a typeahead, pass `variant=typeahead` into the `<MenuToggle>` component.

## Menu Toggle Component
The menu toggle component pairs with the menu OR the panel component to create more customizable dropdown and select implementations. Using a menu toggle with a menu enables you to create custom component configurations not supported by the standard dropdown or select components.

## Menu Component
A menu may contain multiple variations of `<MenuItem>` components. The following example shows a few different states of menu items, where they may use the `itemId` property to link to callbacks. In this example, the `onSelect` property logs information to the console when a menu item is selected. In practice, specific actions can be linked to `onSelect` callbacks.

Additional menu features:
- Use the `hasCheck` property to add a checkbox to a `<MenuItem>`. Use the `isSelected` property to indicate when a `<MenuItem>` is selected.
- Add a `<MenuFooter>` that contains separate, but related actions at the bottom of a menu.
- Add a `<MenuGroup>` to organize `<MenuContent>` and use the `label` property to title a group of menu items. Use the `labelHeadingLevel` property to assign a heading level to the menu group label.

## PatternFly Version
The current release is 6.4.0 with 92 items including components like Button, Dropdown (Beta), Menu, Menu toggle, and more.

## PatternFly Components Overview

### Progress Component
Use a progress bar to keep users informed about how much effort or time they can expect to allocate for long or ongoing processes like loading or updating an app, submitting a form, or completing a multi-step tutorial.

Key guidelines:
- Depending on your use case, you might choose between 2 types of loading indicators: Progress bars and spinners. Never use a progress bar and spinner together.
- If a process takes less than 4 seconds, use a spinner instead.
- Use a toast alert and a progress bar to demonstrate progress during a file download.
- Whenever possible, use a determinate progress bar to communicate progress with measurable values like time or percentage.

### Spinner Component
Position the spinner in the center of the element that is loading. To ensure proper background contrast, use a white or light background.

When to use spinners:
- When the user is advancing to a new page or step where the structure has not been loaded in yet. When the data you are dealing with is unknown in both quantity and shape. When the expected wait time is between 1-5 seconds.
- For experiences longer than 5 seconds, use a progress bar.
- When a loading screen does not have any data, use an empty state screen or failed outcome instead.

### EmptyState Component
Use an empty state component when there is no data or other information to show. It can also be used to communicate completion or other status at the end of a sequential task. An empty state may include a call to action to guide the user on what to do next.(no content)The empty state pattern in PatternFly is "simple enough to use but flexible enough to meet a variety of needs."(no content)PatternFly is Red Hat's open source design system. It consists of components, documentation, and code for building enterprise applications at scale.

For more detailed documentation on PatternFly 6 specifically, you can visit the official PatternFly website at patternfly.org for the latest component documentation.

Here are the search results for **PatternFly 6 Tabs Panel component React**:

## PatternFly Tabs Component

A `<Tabs>` component contains multiple `<Tab>` components that may be used to navigate between sets of content within a page.

### Key Features

**Basic Usage:**
- To label a tab with text, pass a `<TabTitleText>` component into the title property of a `<Tab>`.
- To disable a tab, use the `isDisabled` property.

**Tab Content:**
- To add a content body to a `<TabContent>` component, pass a `<TabContentBody>`. To add padding to the body section, use the `hasPadding` property.

**Expandable Tabs:**
- To flag vertical tabs when they're expanded, use the `isExpanded` property. To flag the default expanded state for uncontrolled tabs, use the `defaultIsExpanded` property.

**Overflow Handling:**
- By default, overflow is applied when there are too many tabs for the width of the container they are in. This overflow can be navigated by side-scrolling within the tabs section, or by selecting the left and right arrows.
- To enable horizontal overflow, use the `isOverflowHorizontal` property.

**Uncontrolled vs Controlled:**
- To allow the `<Tabs>` component to manage setting the active tab and displaying correct content itself, use uncontrolled tabs.

**Mounting Options:**
- To mount tab children (add to the DOM) when a tab is clicked, use the `mountOnEnter` property. Note that this property does not create the tab children until the tab is clicked, so they are not preloaded into the DOM.
- To unmount tab children (remove from the DOM) when they are no longer visible, use the `unmountOnExit` property.

**Closeable and Addable Tabs:**
- To enable closeable tabs, pass the `onClose` property to the `<Tabs>` component. To enable a button that adds new tabs, pass the `onAdd` property to `<Tabs>`.

**Styling Options:**
- Default tabs do not have any borders and use a bottom line to distinguish between a selected tab, a hovered tab, and an inactive tab. Boxed tabs are outlined to emphasize the area that a tab spans.
- To adjust the inset of tabs and visually separate them more, use the `inset` property. You can set the inset to "insetNone", "insetSm", "insetMd", "insetLg", "insetXl", or "inset2xl" at "default", "sm", "md", "lg, "xl, and "2xl" breakpoints.

## Panel Component (Separate Component)

Release 6.3.1 - A panel is a customizable container that can contain other components in flexible content layouts.

Note: The Panel component is a separate component from Tabs in PatternFly 6.

### Resources

- **Official Documentation**: https://www.patternfly.org/components/tabs/
- **Panel Documentation**: https://www.patternfly.org/components/panel/
- **GitHub Source**: https://github.com/patternfly/patternfly-react

## Tarefa Concluida - SPEC-001: Pesquisa do PatternFly 6 Design System

Completei a pesquisa web sobre o PatternFly 6 e salvei todos os resultados em `/Users/anotonio.silva/antonio/mms-last-attempt/subagents/results/20260106-SPEC-001.jsonl`.

### Resumo das Descobertas

**Visao Geral:**
- PatternFly 6 versao 6.4.0 com 92 componentes
- Suporte completo a React 19 (Q2 2025) - nao-breaking, compativel com React 17/18
- Sistema de design tokens com 3 camadas (palette, base, semantic)

**Componentes de Layout:**
- `Page`, `PageSidebar`, `PageSection` - estrutura principal
- `Masthead` - cabecalho com navegacao e toolbar
- `Navigation` - vertical ou horizontal
- `Toolbar` - controles de filtro/acoes
- `Tabs` - navegacao entre conteudos

**Componentes de Dados:**
- `Card` - containers de conteudo (substituiu Tiles)
- `Table` - tabelas composiveis (@patternfly/react-table)
- `Badge`, `Label`, `Icon` - indicadores visuais
- `EmptyState`, `Progress`, `Spinner` - estados de carregamento

**Componentes de Formulario:**
- `Dropdown`, `Select`, `Menu`, `MenuToggle`
- `TextInput`, `TextInputGroup`, `Checkbox`
- `DatePicker`, `Form`, `Button`

**Tema e Tokens:**
- Dark mode via classe `pf-v6-theme-dark` no `<html>`
- Tokens CSS: `--pf-t--global--concept--property--modifier--state`
- Tokens React: `t_global_spacer_sm` em @patternfly/react-tokens
- Breakpoints em rem (nao pixels)

**Pacotes NPM:**
- `@patternfly/react-core@^6` - componentes principais
- `@patternfly/react-table@^6` - tabelas
- `@patternfly/react-icons` - icones
- `@patternfly/react-tokens` - tokens

**Para MMS (Metro Maintenance Scheduler):**
Recomendados: Page+Masthead+Sidebar para layout, Card para visualizacao de tarefas, Table para listas detalhadas, Toolbar para filtros, DatePicker para agendamento, Label/Badge para status.
